Lymphatic malformation is a common congenital abnormality of lymphatic vessel development, typically presenting as cystic vesicles under the skin or mucosa, and can occur at any age. Treatment options include surgical excision, laser therapy, or sclerosing agent injection. Further evaluation is recommended to determine the optimal treatment plan.

On this image, scattered dark red papules and nodules with some crusted erosions are visible on the trunk, consistent with the features of nodular prurigo. Nodular prurigo is a chronic recurrent skin condition characterized by skin rashes, papules, nodules, and itching. The exact cause of nodular prurigo is not yet clear but may be related to immune reactions and genetic factors. Treatment options include topical medications, oral antihistamines, phototherapy, among others. It is recommended for the patient to closely monitor the skin condition, attend regular follow-up visits to adjust the treatment plan.

You may have a sebaceous cyst, which is a common skin condition usually caused by the blockage of the sebaceous gland duct leading to the formation of a subcutaneous cyst. To confirm the diagnosis, it is recommended to undergo a dermatological ultrasound examination. Following a confirmed diagnosis, the doctor can choose an appropriate treatment plan such as surgical excision or other treatment methods.

Based on the medical history and examination results, the patient has a blister with a diameter of 3-4cm below the left hallux and on the dorsum of the foot, with clear fluid inside, indicative of a drug eruption. Drug eruption is a type of allergic reaction to medication, commonly manifested as skin lesions such as rash and blisters after the use of certain drugs. It is recommended to avoid contact with the suspected triggering medication and seek prompt medical treatment.

This is a photo showing multiple cysts on both sides of the labia majora, some of which have ruptured, clinically diagnosed as hidradenitis suppurativa. Hidradenitis suppurativa is an inflammatory disease caused by blockage of sweat gland ducts and bacterial infection, commonly seen in the perineal area. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly to prevent the spread of infection and complications.

This presentation of unevenly colored pigment spots on one side of the nail plate and pigment spots at the nail root is concerning for melanoma. Melanoma is a malignant skin tumor that typically presents as irregular growth of pigmented spots or moles on the skin. It is advisable for the patient to seek medical attention promptly for further evaluation and diagnosis.

Sebaceous nevus is a common skin lesion characterized by yellow-red patches on the scalp or other areas, with a smooth surface and papillary elevations, appearing uneven. These nevi are formed by the proliferation of sebaceous gland tissue, mostly benign and usually do not require treatment. However, regular observation of changes is necessary to rule out the possibility of malignancy.

This is a photo of the forearm showing multiple erythematous patches, papules, and crusts at the site of light exposure, which are typical manifestations of actinic dermatitis, usually caused by sunlight hypersensitivity of the skin. Actinic dermatitis is a common skin condition where patients develop local inflammatory reactions after exposure to ultraviolet light, presenting with symptoms such as erythema, papules, and crusts. Treatment typically involves avoiding sun exposure, using sunscreen, and topical corticosteroids. It is recommended for the patient to avoid direct sunlight exposure, practice daily sun protection, and follow up regularly with a dermatologist for monitoring and treatment.

This is a photo of skin lesions showing extensive erosion with a small amount of exudate. Based on the symptoms and clinical data, the diagnosis is chronic familial benign pemphigus. This disease is a chronic autoimmune disorder characterized by large blisters on the skin and mucous membranes, which are prone to rupture and form ulcers.

Hidrocystoma is a common skin condition characterized by painless, non-itchy small yellowish to flesh-colored papules scattered on the skin surface. It is usually caused by obstruction of the eccrine ducts in the skin and often occurs on the face, neck, chest, and other areas. Hidrocystomas are typically benign and do not usually require specific treatment, but if there are cosmetic concerns or discomfort, laser therapy or surgical treatment may be considered.

Keratotic papules. Consideration: Common warts.Keratotic papules are a common skin lesion, typically presenting as small raised bumps on the skin, often found on the hands, feet, and other parts of the body. Common warts are viral skin infections caused by human papillomavirus, commonly found on the hands and soles of the feet, characterized by rough, grayish-white wart-like lesions. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

This is a photo of the sole of the foot, showing irregular papules, nodules, and plaques that are partially fused and appear dark red. Based on the characteristics of the lesion, along with clinical presentation and histopathological examination results, the diagnosis is Kaposi's sarcoma, a malignant soft tissue tumor that typically occurs in the skin and mucous membranes. This type of tumor often has an invasive nature and requires a comprehensive approach to treatment, including surgical excision, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy.

In a patient presenting with purple patches on the trunk with red raised edges, this may be a symptom of purpura. Purpura is a hemorrhagic disease commonly seen on the skin and mucous membranes, characterized by purple patches, ecchymosis, or petechiae, often with raised red edges. To confirm the diagnosis, it is necessary to combine the patient's medical history and perform further auxiliary examinations, such as skin biopsy.

The diagnosis is neutrophilic dermatosis, a rare chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by multiple round red papules and plaques, commonly found on the trunk and upper limbs. Based on the characteristics of the lesion shown in the photo, the preliminary diagnosis is congenital melanocytic nevus. In most cases, these nevi are benign and do not require special treatment, but regular monitoring for changes is still important.

This is a photo of the lower limbs and external genitalia of a child, showing multiple erosions, erythema, irregular patches, crusts, and scales. Based on the characteristic presentation, along with clinical symptoms and medical history, the diagnosis is consistent with pyoderma gangrenosum.

This is a photo of a rash in the armpit area, showing several red papules and nodules, consistent with the clinical presentation of hidradenitis suppurativa. Hidradenitis suppurativa is an infectious disease of the sweat glands, commonly found in areas such as the armpits and groin, with the main cause being related to bacterial infection. Treatment includes topical antibiotic creams and maintaining local cleanliness and dryness.

Based on the photo showing a large ulcer with irregular edges and purulent discharge at the foreskin of the penis, the diagnosis is consistent with Behcet's disease. Behcet's disease is a chronic recurrent autoimmune disorder characterized by chronic non-specific inflammation and ulcers on mucous membranes and skin. Common manifestations include genital ulcers, uveitis, oral ulcers, and more. Treatment often involves the use of steroids, immunosuppressants, and other medications to control the symptoms.

Red lobulated nodules on the face may be a manifestation of pyogenic granuloma in the skin. Granuloma is a non-infectious inflammatory lesion, usually caused by the tissue's response to external stimuli (such as foreign bodies, infections, drugs, etc.) . This condition usually requires a skin biopsy to confirm the diagnosis, and treatment options are determined based on the severity of the lesion and clinical symptoms.

This is an image showing skin lesions on the face and hands of the patient, with symptoms like erythema, erosion, ulceration, and crusting around the mouth and fingertips, which are typical manifestations of erythema perstans crohn's disease. Erythema perstans crohn's disease is a rare chronic inflammatory disease that mainly affects the skin of the distal extremities. Common symptoms include erythema, exudation, crusting, and itching, severely impacting the patient's quality of life. Treatment usually involves a combination of topical medications and oral anti-inflammatory drugs.

On this photo of the face, we can see scattered red papules with irregular shapes and partial fusion, which are characteristic of nodular disease. These findings, combined with clinical symptoms and signs, support the diagnosis of nodular disease. Nodular disease is a chronic non-caseating inflammatory condition that can affect various organs in the body, commonly found in lymph nodes, lungs, eyes, and skin. Treatment usually involves monitoring, medication, and sometimes surgical intervention when necessary.

This is a chronic autoimmune disease that usually presents as annular erythematous plaques on the skin with slightly raised edges, smooth surfaces, and sometimes accompanied by slight scaling. The pathogenesis of annular erythema is not fully understood but may be related to abnormal activation of the immune system. Treatment mainly focuses on symptomatic local medication and immunomodulatory therapy.

This is a picture of skin lesions on the hand, showing redness, papules, vesicles, and burrows between the fingers, which are typical manifestations of scabies. Scabies is a parasitic skin disease caused by infestation with the mite Sarcoptes scabiei. It commonly affects areas like fingers and wrists, and patients often experience itching. Diagnosing scabies requires a combination of clinical symptoms and medical history, and it can be confirmed through skin scraping examination.

This is a common skin condition, usually caused by the deposition of skin pigments. Pigmented nevi are usually benign and do not require treatment in most cases. However, if there are any changes in shape, color, or borders, medical evaluation should be sought promptly to rule out serious skin conditions such as malignant melanoma.

A diagnosis of juvenile xanthogranuloma is made based on the observation of a pale red nodule with some scales on the surface. Juvenile xanthogranuloma is a rare chronic non-specific inflammatory disease characterized by yellow or orange nodules on the skin or mucous membranes, often accompanied by scales and bleeding. The etiology of juvenile xanthogranuloma is not clear, but it may be related to factors such as genetics and immune abnormalities. Clinically, juvenile xanthogranuloma often needs to be differentiated from other diseases such as sarcoidosis, lupus erythematosus, etc. Treatment may involve the use of steroids, immunosuppressants, and other medications, but the effectiveness varies from person to person. Patients need regular follow-up visits to monitor the condition and guide treatment.

The description of purplish-red papules with smooth surface is consistent with the characteristics of hemangioma. Hemangioma is a common vascular malformation disease, usually presenting as red or purplish-red papules on the skin with a smooth surface. Hemangiomas can be classified into congenital and acquired types, and are typically benign. For the diagnosis and treatment of hemangiomas, further skin biopsy may be considered to confirm the diagnosis, and appropriate treatment options can be chosen based on the size, location, and symptoms of the lesion.

Based on the characteristics of the lesions, the diagnosis is epidermolysis bullosa. Epidermolysis bullosa is a rare genetic disorder characterized by fragile skin that blisters and forms ulcers easily, leading to scars. Treatment mainly focuses on maintaining skin hygiene, preventing infections, and sometimes requires topical medications or other therapeutic interventions.

This is a photo of a lesion on the frontal area, displaying a single nodular growth with well-defined borders. In the center, there is an ulceration with reddish-brown crust, and the edges appear raised like a string of beads. These features are consistent with basal cell carcinoma, a common type of skin cancer that typically grows slowly but can invade deeper tissues if not treated promptly.

This is an image showing multiple pale red patches on the face, varying in size with clear borders and visible dilated capillaries. Based on the characteristics of the lesions and clinical symptoms, a preliminary diagnosis of lupus erythematosus is made. Lupus erythematosus is a chronic autoimmune disease that mainly affects the skin and multiple organ systems, presenting with symptoms such as skin rashes, arthritis, and kidney damage. Treatment should be tailored to the individual patient, considering both local and systemic therapies to control the disease and alleviate symptoms.

On this photo of a child's face, we can observe erythema, exudation, and partial loss of skin pigmentation, which are consistent with the diagnosis of lupus erythematosus. Lupus erythematosus is a chronic autoimmune disease commonly affecting the face, neck, and hands, leading to skin inflammation and abnormal pigmentation. Treatment typically involves the use of anti-inflammatory medications and immunosuppressants. It is a condition that requires long-term management, and patients need regular follow-up appointments to monitor the condition and adjust the treatment plan.

This is a picture of skin lesions on the shoulder of the patient, showing multiple purplish-red patches merging into large areas. Based on the appearance and distribution of the lesions, along with clinical symptoms, the diagnosis is likely subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus. Lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune disease commonly seen in young women, characterized by skin manifestations such as red patches, rashes, and photosensitivity. Treatment typically involves a combination of corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, and other medications.

Pediculosis is a parasitic disease caused by lice infesting the human skin. Patients often present with symptoms such as skin erythema, papules, wheals, scratch marks, and scabs. In severe infections, symptoms such as head itching and visible parasites may also occur. Diagnosis and differentiation can be made by observing lice or their eggs with the naked eye.

Diagnosis of possible scabies based on the irregular red patches with varying sizes of vesicles seen in the image. Scabies is a parasitic infection caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei, characterized by localized skin redness, itching, and vesicles. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

This photo shows isolated black macules with uniform color and clear borders from the surrounding skin, consistent with a melanocytic nevus. Melanocytic nevi are common skin lesions, usually resulting from the overgrowth of pigment-producing cells in the skin. In most cases, melanocytic nevi are benign and do not require specific treatment, but any changes should be promptly evaluated and monitored by a healthcare professional.

This is an image of skin lesions on the wrist, showing linear and tentacle-like distribution of erythema with varying sizes of blisters. Based on the characteristics of the lesions, the possible diagnosis is contact dermatitis caused by contact with certain substances such as coral, jellyfish, or hidden bugs. Contact dermatitis is a type of skin inflammation usually triggered by an allergic reaction to specific substances that the skin comes into contact with, presenting symptoms like rash, itching, and blisters.

The patient presents with symptoms of follicular perifollicular erythema and telangiectasia. Based on clinical manifestations and physical examinations, the diagnosis is folliculitis. Folliculitis is a common skin condition often caused by bacterial infection, leading to inflamed and painful hair follicles. Treatment typically involves topical antibiotics and maintaining skin hygiene.

This is an image showing skin lesions on both upper limbs, characterized by dark red patches with clear borders, and various shiny flat papules of different sizes. Based on the characteristics of the lesions, the diagnosis is flat warts.

This is a photo showing diffuse large red patches with white scales on the trunk of the patient, which is a typical presentation of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune skin disease characterized by dry, red, scaly skin patches that significantly impact the patient's quality of life. Treatment may involve topical medications, phototherapy, oral medications, etc., but the disease often recurs and requires long-term regular management.

Hyaline mucinosis is a rare skin condition characterized by pale red, wax-like shiny papules on the skin, sometimes merging into plaques. The specific cause of this disease is not clear, but it may be related to immune system abnormalities. Treatment options include topical corticosteroid creams, phototherapy, and oral medications. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for controlling the progression of the disease.

This photo shows pale red patches with thin white scales on the skin of the hand, which is a typical presentation of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune skin disease characterized by red patches covered with silvery white scales on the skin surface. The etiology of the disease is complex and may be related to factors such as genetics and immune system abnormalities. Treatment options are diverse, including topical medications, phototherapy, and oral medications. Early medical consultation for diagnosis and treatment is recommended.

The photo shows a purplish-red lump on the patient's cheek, and based on the characteristics and manifestations of the lesion, the preliminary diagnosis is sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis is a chronic non-caseating granulomatous disease that can affect various organs in the body. It commonly occurs in the lungs and lymph nodes, but can also develop in areas such as the face. The etiology of this disease is unknown, and diagnosis usually requires tests such as tissue biopsy. Treatment options include medications such as steroids and immunomodulators.

The image shows linearly arranged small papules in the corona of the glans penis, with a skin-colored or whitish appearance. This condition is diagnosed as pearly penile papules, a common benign finding that usually does not require treatment. Regular observation is recommended to monitor any changes, while avoiding over-cleaning or irritation. If there is any pain, ulceration, itching, or other discomfort, prompt medical attention should be sought for further evaluation and management.

Smooth nodule with a smooth surface and regular margins. This presentation typically suggests a benign lesion, but further investigations are needed to rule out malignancy. Considering the patient's medical history and clinical symptoms, a differential diagnosis for scar-related nodules is warranted.

This is a common skin lesion, usually black or brown in color, presenting as macules with a darker central area and papillary-like projections on the surface. This characteristic lesion is diagnosed as a melanocytic nevus, which is typically a benign condition. In melanocytic nevi, there is proliferation of nevus cells in the epidermis and dermis, forming clusters of melanocytes. Treatment is usually not necessary, but regular monitoring to ensure no malignant transformation is important.

The photo shows widespread red papules on the chest and upper limbs, which, combined with clinical symptoms and characteristics of skin lesions, can be preliminarily diagnosed as scabies. Scabies is a contagious skin disease caused by the parasitic mite Sarcoptes scabiei on the skin surface, often presenting with itching, redness, papules, and vesicles. Confirmation of the diagnosis and appropriate treatment measures should be taken through clinical and laboratory examinations.

The patient presents with erythematous papules on the trunk along with linear/circular changes, suggesting a possible skin infection. However, further investigation including detailed medical history and laboratory tests are needed to confirm the diagnosis.

The appearance of discolored patches with scaling on the neck is a common symptom of skin diseases, possibly caused by dermatitis. Possible diagnoses include eczema, psoriasis, and other inflammatory skin conditions, which require further examination and confirmation.

Linear porokeratosis is a common skin condition characterized by small, rough, circular or polygonal papules on the skin surface, commonly found on the face, chest, and back. The exact cause of this condition is not clear, but it may be related to genetic factors, sun exposure, and skin dryness. Treatment options may include keratolytic agents, topical keratolytic agents, and laser therapy.

Blackhead comedone is a common skin condition characterized by visible clustered follicular papules with blackheads at the follicle openings. This condition typically occurs on the face, neck, and back, and is more common in adolescents and young adults. The formation of blackhead comedones is related to the blockage of follicular secretions at the follicle opening, leading to the formation of oxidized blackheads. Treatment usually involves regular skin cleansing, the use of cosmetics or medications containing salicylic acid, and in severe cases, seeking medical attention for further treatment.

The presentation of triangular brownish patches with irregular black papules on the surface is consistent with melanoma. Melanoma is a common malignant skin tumor that is usually black or brown, with irregular shape and blurred borders. Upon identification of melanoma lesions, prompt medical attention for further diagnosis and treatment is advised.

Linear keratosis is a rare skin disease characterized by the presence of keratotic papules with rough surfaces on the skin. This condition may cause dryness, itching, and discomfort. Treatment typically involves topical medications and moisturizing measures. In some cases, light therapy can also be used to treat linear keratosis.

On this facial photo, there are red patches with unclear borders and thin scales on the cheeks, consistent with the clinical presentation of actinic dermatitis. Actinic dermatitis is a skin inflammation caused by prolonged exposure to ultraviolet light, commonly seen on sun-exposed areas of the face. Treatment involves avoiding sunlight exposure, along with the consideration of topical corticosteroids and anti-inflammatory medications. Regular follow-up visits to monitor the progression of the condition are also crucial.

This is a picture of skin lesion on the shoulder and back, showing large patches of erythema with varying degrees of desquamation. Based on the symptoms and features in the picture, the preliminary diagnosis is sunburn. Sunburn is caused by prolonged exposure to ultraviolet rays, leading to inflammatory reactions in the skin, commonly seen in individuals who are exposed to excessive sunlight. Treatment includes avoiding prolonged sun exposure, using topical anti-inflammatory medications, and keeping the skin clean and moisturized.

The photo shows a single red patch on the earlobe, with ulceration and ulceration in the center, and swelling around it, based on the characteristics and manifestations of the lesion, clinically diagnosed as basal cell carcinoma.

This is a picture showing skin lesions on the hand, with dark red patches visible on the wrist, some areas appearing black, sometimes with scabs visible, clear borders, and based on the characteristics of the lesion, the preliminary diagnosis is basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that usually grows slowly, but if not treated promptly, it may invade surrounding tissues.

This is a photo showing multiple blisters, pigment loss spots, and scabs on the hand, which are consistent with the clinical features of porphyria cutanea tarda. Porphyria cutanea tarda is a rare genetic disorder characterized by abnormal iron metabolism in the body, leading to photosensitivity of the skin and the presentation of blisters, pigmentation, and scabs.

There was a flaky purple-red plaque on the right cheek, with an uneven surface, dilated capillaries, and raised and ulcerated edges. In line with the clinical manifestations of lupus erythematosus, the diagnosis requires a combination of immunological examination and skin histopathological examination.

Based on the presence of oval-shaped dark red patches with nodules of varying sizes on the skin, along with the clinical symptoms, the preliminary diagnosis is nodular disease. This rare chronic inflammatory condition is characterized by the formation of granulomatous nodules in the skin, mucous membranes, and internal organs. Treatment typically involves the use of steroids, immunosuppressants, and in severe cases, surgical intervention.

Brown irregular patches with mild elevation and rough texture are typical features of seborrheic keratosis. This is a common skin condition usually caused by excessive keratinization of the skin. Seborrheic keratosis commonly appears in areas exposed to sunlight, such as the face, neck, and back. A definitive diagnosis of seborrheic keratosis typically requires further examination with tools like dermoscopy to help the physician confirm the condition.

This is a photo of a skin lesion on the head, showing a single flat pale yellow patch with smooth surface and raised beaded edges. Based on the characteristics of the lesion, the patient has been diagnosed with basal cell carcinoma, which is a common type of skin cancer that usually grows slowly but can be locally invasive.

Erythema multiforme is a common skin condition characterized by scattered ring-shaped red patches on smooth skin, with a target-like appearance in the center. One of the features of this condition is its diverse morphology and distribution of rashes, hence the name "multiforme." Erythema multiforme is usually caused by allergic reactions or infections, and can be managed with antihistamines, topical steroids, and other treatment options to alleviate symptoms.

A mucocele is a common oral soft tissue tumor, typically formed due to the blockage of salivary glands in the lips. Mucoceles are usually painless but can cause swelling and discomfort in the lips. Treatment for mucoceles includes surgical removal of the cyst and ensuring oral hygiene to prevent infection and recurrence.

This is a description of a skin lesion, characterized by dome-shaped papules with slightly darker pigmentation at the edges. The possibility of a melanocytic nevus is considered, but further evaluation with dermatoscopy is needed for a definitive diagnosis.

At the nasolabial fold, there are red papules. Consider: granuloma. Granuloma is a non-infectious inflammatory lesion, typically caused by the aggregation of inflammatory cells within tissues, and can occur in any part of the body. The etiology of granuloma is diverse, possibly due to infection, autoimmune factors, or other inflammatory factors. The diagnosis of granuloma usually requires a combination of the patient's clinical symptoms, medical history, and histopathological manifestations of the lesion. The treatment of granuloma depends on the location of the lesion, the severity, and the overall health of the patient, with common treatment modalities including medication, surgical excision, or other interventional therapies.

The photo shows a light brown patch with unclear borders and relatively regular shape, which could be a dysplastic nevus on the skin. Dysplastic nevi are usually benign skin lesions, but it is important to monitor changes regularly and follow medical advice for further evaluation.

This photo shows a round, bright red nodule protruding from the skin surface, with a smooth surface, consistent with the clinical presentation of a hemangioma. Hemangiomas are a common vascular developmental abnormality, often caused by proliferation of endothelial cells. Hemangiomas are usually benign, but may require treatment in some cases, such as if they cause bleeding, pain, or affect appearance. Treatment options for hemangiomas include laser therapy, surgical excision, and medication.

This photo shows a large red-colored mass with scales on the surface, which, combined with clinical symptoms and signs, is suggestive of lymphoma. Lymphoma is a malignant tumor of the lymphoid tissue, classified into Hodgkin lymphoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma, with different subtypes and clinical manifestations. The diagnosis of lymphoma requires a comprehensive evaluation of medical history, clinical presentation, imaging studies, and histopathological findings. Treatment options are determined based on the severity of the condition and the overall health of the patient, which may include chemotherapy, radiotherapy, targeted therapy, or immunotherapy.
This image shows keratinized plaques distributed along the long axis of the finger, showing characteristics of verrucous growths, a condition commonly referred to as epidermal nevus.

Eye lid pediculosis is an eye infection disease caused by parasites, characterized by symptoms such as eyelid redness, itching, inflammation, and possible visible lice. The diagnosis is usually confirmed through eye examination, and treatment includes local medication or oral medication. The key to preventing eye lid pediculosis is good personal hygiene and avoiding close contact with infected individuals.

Based on the clinical presentation and characteristics of the skin lesions observed, the patient presents with brownish flat papules with uneven color and irregular surface, without hyperkeratosis or scales. Given these symptoms, further consideration is needed for the diagnosis of melanocytic nevus, seborrheic keratosis, and melanoma.

The photo shows a solitary nodule with a central ulceration and peripheral elevation, consistent with clinical features of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is one of the most common types of skin cancer, typically occurring on the head and neck, especially in sun-exposed areas. This type of cancer usually grows slowly but may invade deeper tissues if not treated promptly. Treatment options include surgical excision, radiation therapy, and topical medication.

This is a photo of a male patient's face, showing diffuse red hyperplasia on the nose. Based on the characteristics and presentation of the lesions, the diagnosis is rhinophyma, also known as rosacea nose. Rhinophyma is a common skin condition typically caused by factors such as blocked pores, abnormal sebaceous gland secretion, and chronic inflammation. Symptoms include red pimples, dilated blood vessels, and increased sebum production on the nose area. Treatment for rhinophyma often involves topical medication, laser therapy, or surgical intervention.

Trichostasis spinulosa is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by follicular, firm, small papules, typically on the face and around the eyebrows, sometimes accompanied by itching and scaling. The etiology may be related to fungal infection and abnormal sebaceous gland function. Treatment often includes topical antifungal agents, keratolytic agents, and sebum control. Regular cleansing of facial skin, avoiding oily foods, and managing stress can help alleviate symptoms.

Multiple target-like erythematous patches scattered on the palms are consistent with the clinical features of erythema multiforme. Erythema multiforme is a common skin condition characterized by irregularly shaped, varying-sized erythematous patches or plaques on the skin, often accompanied by itching or burning sensation. The etiology may be related to factors such as infections, drug allergies, or immune abnormalities. Treatment typically involves topical medications and oral antihistamines. Early detection and treatment can help alleviate symptoms and prevent disease progression.

This is a picture of skin lesions on the abdomen, showing infiltrative erythema with silvery-white scales, indicative of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic relapsing skin disease commonly seen on the scalp, trunk, and limbs, with a complex etiology involving genetic, immune, and environmental factors. Typical symptoms include erythema, scales, and itching, which can significantly impact the patient's quality of life and require timely diagnosis and treatment.

The photo of the left side of the face of this boy shows many papules, most of which are red and some are white. Based on the symptoms and the appearance in the photo, the preliminary diagnosis is acne. Acne is a common skin condition, often occurring during adolescence, caused by the blockage of hair follicles due to excessive sebum production by the sebaceous glands. It is recommended to maintain facial cleanliness regularly, avoid squeezing the papules, and consider seeking medical advice for further evaluation and treatment.

There are papules on the cheeks with the same color as the skin, which could be indicative of various skin conditions such as acne, eczema, or allergic reactions. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for a detailed examination to confirm the diagnosis and develop an effective treatment plan.

Based on the description and appearance of scattered red papules and patches with some scales on the wrist, the diagnosis of scabies is made. Scabies is an infectious skin condition caused by the infestation of the human itch mite, leading to symptoms such as itching, rash, and inflammatory reactions. Diagnosis of scabies is typically done through skin examination and symptom observation, with treatment options involving topical medications and personal hygiene management.

This is an image showing facial skin lesions with erosions on the upper and lower lips, along with crust formation. With the involvement of mucous membranes in the eyes, the clinical presentation is consistent with Stevens-Johnson syndrome. This is a severe hypersensitivity reaction commonly triggered by drug allergies, which can lead to multi-organ damage in severe cases, requiring urgent management and monitoring.

Rosacea is a common chronic skin condition characterized by the presence of circular red patches on the skin, with a central fading and a rose-colored edge. This condition may be accompanied by mild itching or a burning sensation. The exact cause of rosacea is not yet clear but is believed to be related to abnormalities in the immune system. Treatment typically involves topical medications to alleviate symptoms, and sometimes oral antifungal medications may be necessary. Timely diagnosis and treatment can effectively relieve symptoms and prevent recurrence.

On this photo of the trunk, red scaly papules and partial desquamation are observed, consistent with the diagnosis of dermatomyositis. Dermatomyositis is an autoimmune disease characterized by skin inflammation and muscle weakness. It can affect other parts of the body such as the hands, feet, and facial muscles, and may even lead to systemic complications. Treatment typically involves anti-inflammatory medications and immunosuppressants.

This is a picture of a pyogenic granuloma on the lower lip, presenting as a spherical lesion with some yellow crust on the surface, commonly found in oral mucosal tissues and usually caused by bacterial infection. It is recommended to perform bacterial culture and sensitivity testing to identify the specific bacteria causing the infection and choose appropriate antibiotics for treatment.

The photo shows multiple oval dark red patches with collarette scales, which are typical manifestations of pityriasis rosea. Pityriasis rosea is a chronic and recurrent skin condition characterized by red patches and scales, commonly seen in middle-aged and elderly individuals, especially women. The exact cause is unclear but may be related to factors such as genetics and immunity. Treatment typically involves topical corticosteroids, oral antihistamines, etc. Regular follow-up and avoidance of triggering factors are crucial for disease control.

This is a photo showing a single ulcer on the frontal area, with a reddish-brown crust and bleeding in the center, and slightly raised bead-like edges, which are consistent with the characteristics of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically grows slowly but can spread and cause more serious issues if not treated promptly.

It is a description of a skin lesion, indicating the presence of uneven dark red nodules on the skin surface, possibly suggesting hypertrophic scars. Hypertrophic scars are nodular skin lesions formed by excessive healing of scars, usually occurring at the site of trauma, appearing as uneven nodules with a dark red color. Diagnosis of hypertrophic scars requires a comprehensive analysis based on the patient's medical history, clinical manifestations, and characteristics of the skin lesions, sometimes requiring further skin biopsy for confirmation.

Juvenile xanthogranuloma is a common skin condition in infants and young children, typically presenting as red or yellow papules with diameters ranging from a few millimeters to several centimeters. This condition is usually benign and often occurs on the face, neck, and trunk. The exact cause of juvenile xanthogranuloma is not fully understood but may be related to genetics, immunity, and inflammation. Treatment is generally not required as the symptoms tend to resolve on their own within weeks to months.

Erythema annulare centrifugum is a common skin condition characterized by central clearing of the lesion with a raised erythematous border, forming a ring-shaped rash. It is often caused by fungal infections, particularly dermatophytes. Treatment typically involves topical antifungal medications such as ketoconazole. Timely diagnosis and treatment are crucial in preventing the spread and recurrence of the lesions.

The image shows a reddish mass with clear borders, semi-spherical in shape, consistent with the clinical presentation of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically grows slowly and rarely metastasizes, but if left untreated, it can invade surrounding tissues and organs. Treatment options include surgical excision, cryotherapy, radiation therapy, among others.

These are photos of the upper limbs showing large areas of erythema, papules, erosion, and exudation symmetrically distributed, consistent with eczema. Eczema is a chronic relapsing inflammatory skin disease that commonly occurs in individuals with dry skin, allergic tendencies, or immune dysfunction. Treatment may involve the use of topical corticosteroids, moisturizing, and avoidance of triggering factors.

This is a photo showing multiple smooth light brown nodules on the chest and breast area, with irregular shapes and some resembling crab legs, protruding from the skin surface. Based on the characteristics of the lesions, the most likely diagnosis is hypertrophic scar. This condition is typically caused by excessive scar tissue formation following trauma, surgery, or other skin injuries. Further skin biopsy is recommended for confirmation of the diagnosis.

Based on the description of the medical history, the patient presents with scattered brown, round patches, suggestive of tinea versicolor. Tinea versicolor is a skin condition caused by fungal infection, commonly found on the scalp, neck, and trunk, characterized by brown, round patches with well-defined borders, sometimes accompanied by mild itching. Diagnosis usually involves fungal culture or skin scraping examination.

This is an image showing skin lesions on the hands, with multiple purple-red spots on the palms and fingers, consistent with a diagnosis of subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus. Subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus is a subtype of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), characterized by more severe skin involvement compared to organ involvement. It typically presents with skin rashes, photosensitivity, joint pain, among other symptoms, and requires prompt medical attention and treatment.

This is a photo showing large areas of brown patches on the patient's lower back, with dryness and scaling. Based on the characteristics of the lesion, a preliminary diagnosis of fungal granuloma is made. Fungal granuloma is a skin condition caused by fungal infection, commonly seen in patients with immunodeficiency diseases.

Based on the description and appearance of the lesion in the photo, it is diagnosed as a melanocytic nevus. Melanocytic nevi are common benign skin lesions that are typically asymptomatic and do not require treatment, but regular monitoring for changes is important.

Based on the description provided, the patient may have malignant melanoma. Melanoma is a malignant tumor commonly found on the skin, characterized by dark brownish-black, irregularly colored, unevenly shaped, and uneven surfaced nodules. Malignant melanoma is known for its aggressive nature and rapid metastasis, making early detection and treatment crucial. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for further evaluation and management.

This is an image of a nasal skin lesion showing ulceration or plaques with pearly borders, highly suggestive of basal cell carcinoma, especially the sclerosing type. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that usually grows slowly, but if left untreated, it can invade deeper tissues. It is recommended to seek prompt medical attention for further diagnosis and treatment.

Based on the appearance of the yellow-brown oval-shaped nodules isolated on the skin of the limbs in the photo, with a smooth surface, the diagnosis of skin fibroma is made. Skin fibroma is a common benign skin tumor, typically characterized by the proliferation of fibrous tissue in the dermis. These tumors are usually painless, slow-growing, and commonly seen in adults. Treatment typically involves surgical removal, with a generally good prognosis.

This is a photo of a finger showing two water blisters with clear fluid. Combined with the symptoms appearing after sun exposure, the preliminary diagnosis is likely to be solar dermatitis. Solar dermatitis is a condition caused by skin sensitivity to ultraviolet light, commonly seen in sun-exposed areas, presenting symptoms such as blisters, erythema, and itching. Disseminated actinic porokeratosis is a rare skin condition characterized by red papules and plaques with excessive keratinization, typically appearing in areas exposed to ultraviolet radiation. These skin lesions may be surrounded by dark brown rings and exhibit symmetric distribution. The exact cause of the disease is not fully understood, but genetic factors and UV radiation may play a role. Treatment usually involves topical keratolytics, laser therapy, and phototherapy.

In a patient, there are brown patches with uneven papules and nodules on the inside of the thigh, clustered together, suggesting a skin tumor. A histopathological examination is recommended to confirm the diagnosis, which can help determine the nature and type of the lesion and guide further treatment.

This photo shows irregular white patches on the trunk of the patient, consistent with the clinical presentation of vitiligo. Vitiligo is a common chronic autoimmune disease characterized by damage to the skin's pigment cells, leading to localized or widespread depigmentation resulting in white patches. Treatment options may include laser therapy, phototherapy, topical corticosteroids, among others. Timely diagnosis and treatment are crucial for controlling disease progression and improving the patient's quality of life.
This photo shows erythematous plaques with dense yellow pustules on the lower limbs, some of which have merged into pustular lakes, consistent with a diagnosis of pustular psoriasis.

The photo shows dense distribution of pale yellow papules on the patient's back with evident keratosis, which is indicative of a diagnosis of lichen planus. Lichen planus is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by flat, keratotic patches or papules on the skin. This condition is usually non-infectious but can persist, requiring prompt medical attention and treatment.

Based on the described symptoms, erysipelas is considered. Erysipelas is a common skin infectious disease characterized by local redness, swelling, warmth, and skin surface thickening with scales. A definitive diagnosis of erysipelas requires a comprehensive evaluation of medical history, clinical manifestations, and laboratory tests such as bacterial culture. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly and undergo further examinations for a confirmed diagnosis.

This is a photo of the back of the hand, showing multiple round red papules. Combined with the clinical presentation and medical history, it is consistent with the features of nodular disease. Nodular disease is a systemic disease that can affect multiple organs, commonly involving the skin, eyes, lymph nodes, and lungs. Treatment typically involves a combination of medication and addressing the underlying cause. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for further evaluation and management.

This is an image showing skin lesions on both lower limbs, presenting as swollen dark red patches with clear borders. Based on the symptoms, a preliminary diagnosis of sclerosing edema is made. Sclerosing edema is a rare skin condition characterized by localized skin edema, hardening, and mucous cysts, often accompanied by redness and pain. Definitive diagnosis requires a combination of clinical symptoms, medical history, and possible laboratory test results. Treatment focuses on symptom relief and disease progression control.

This is a photo of the hand showing multiple erythematous annular lesions, indicative of erythema multiforme. Erythema multiforme presents as red, slightly raised annular rashes, commonly seen on the hands and fingers. It is a self-limiting condition that typically does not require specific treatment, but avoiding triggering factors is important.

Neurofibromatosis is a common neurogenic tumor that typically presents as multiple neurofibromas. The lesions often appear as densely distributed pink, soft pedunculated nodules of varying sizes. These nodules are usually benign but can also potentially undergo malignant transformation into malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors in some cases. Diagnosis is typically based on clinical presentation, medical history, imaging studies, and histopathological findings. Treatment options depend on the location, size, and symptoms of the tumor, and may include surgical excision, radiation therapy, and medical management.

The photo shows red, thickened, and edematous patches on the exposed areas of both upper limbs, consistent with the presentation of actinic dermatitis. Actinic dermatitis is a skin inflammatory disease caused by long-term exposure to ultraviolet light, commonly seen on sun-exposed areas such as the face, neck, and upper limbs. Treatment mainly involves avoiding UV exposure, topical medication, and moisturizing care. It is recommended for the patient to avoid sun exposure, use sunscreen and protective clothing, and seek medical treatment promptly.

This facial photo shows symmetrical distribution of erythematous patches with yellow scales, consistent with the features of seborrheic dermatitis. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common chronic inflammatory skin condition that typically occurs on the face, scalp, eyebrows, and around the eyelashes. It is often associated with factors such as excessive sebum production and fungal infections. Treatment usually involves topical medications such as keratolytics and antifungals, along with maintaining good skin hygiene. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and management.

This is a photo showing extensive erythematous plaques with central atrophy on the scalp, typical of lupus erythematosus. Lupus erythematosus is a chronic autoimmune disease that can affect multiple organ systems. Common skin manifestations include malar rash, discoid rash, and photosensitivity. Treatment typically involves skin care, sun protection, anti-inflammatory medications, and immunosuppressants.

On this photo of the trunk, dry skin, prominent keratosis, and deepened skin texture are visible, indicating a diagnosis of psoriasis vulgaris. Psoriasis vulgaris is a chronic, non-infectious skin disease characterized by excessive growth of the skin keratin layer, leading to dry skin and increased scaling. It commonly occurs on the trunk, limbs, and other areas. Treatment mainly involves topical medications such as moisturizers and keratolytics, while severe cases may require oral medications.

The photo shows the uneven nail plate and scales around the nail, typical manifestations of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory skin disease that commonly affects the skin and nails. Nail changes can be a characteristic feature of psoriasis, often accompanied by symptoms such as nail cracking, thickening, or discoloration. In diagnosing psoriasis, in addition to nail symptoms, a comprehensive assessment based on systemic symptoms and other skin manifestations is needed.

Multiple irregularly shaped, slightly red papules and plaques on the trunk of the patient suggest the need for a histopathological examination to establish a definite diagnosis. Differential diagnoses to consider may include skin conditions such as psoriasis, eczema, or skin cancer. Prompt medical attention is advised for further evaluation and treatment.

The photo shows a pale red flat lesion on the back of the hand, with a rough surface and a thin layer of scales, consistent with the characteristics of in situ squamous cell carcinoma. In situ squamous cell carcinoma is a precancerous lesion that usually grows slowly, with locally invasive growth and a potential risk of progressing to squamous cell carcinoma. Further histopathological examination is recommended to confirm the diagnosis and to develop an appropriate treatment plan.
Patients present with reddish-brown lichenified papules and some lesions with vesicles at the edges, which are consistent with the clinical features of chronic lichenified eczema.

Based on the presentation of red papules with pustules on the cheeks, the likely diagnosis is acne. Acne is a common chronic skin condition that typically begins in adolescence and is characterized by the presence of comedones, papules, and pustules around the hair follicles on the face, chest, and back. Treatment options may include topical medications, oral medications, or other therapeutic interventions, along with maintaining good skin hygiene.

In this case, the multiple red papules inside the auricle may be due to furuncle. Furuncle is a common skin infection typically caused by Staphylococcus aureus. This condition often presents with redness, swelling, pain, warmth, and pus drainage. Further investigations such as bacterial culture or other tests may be needed for a definitive diagnosis. Treatment usually involves topical antibiotic ointment or oral antibiotics, as well as keeping the area clean and dry.

This is a photo showing facial skin lesions characterized by erythema with inflammatory papules and telangiectasia, consistent with the clinical features of rosacea. Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory skin condition mainly presenting with facial erythema, papules, and telangiectasia, often accompanied by flushing and acne-like lesions. Treatment typically involves topical medications and oral antibiotics.

This is a photo of a woman's face showing an irregularly shaped brown patch with oozing in the center. Based on these features, the diagnosis is malignant melanoma. Melanoma is a malignant skin tumor that typically presents as dark-colored or brown patches on the skin, sometimes with irregular borders and color variations, and may have ulcers in the center. Melanoma has the potential for malignant metastasis, so prompt medical attention and treatment are essential.

Multiple black pedunculated growths of varying sizes in the neck are consistent with clinical manifestations of skin tags. Skin tags are common benign skin tumors that typically appear as black or brown protruding lumps on the skin surface, commonly found in areas such as the neck, armpits, and groin. In most cases, skin tags are harmless and do not require treatment, but if there are symptoms such as pain, bleeding, rapid growth, prompt medical attention should be sought for further evaluation and management.

This is an image showing diffuse dark red papules on the face, with subsequent pigmentation loss after healing, consistent with the diagnosis of lupus erythematosus. Lupus erythematosus is a chronic autoimmune disease that often affects the skin and multiple organ systems, presenting with a variety of clinical manifestations. Diagnosis relies on clinical presentation and laboratory tests, and treatment involves the use of steroids and immunosuppressants for control.

In this photograph, a light brown to dark brown patch with a rough, wart-like surface that protrudes from the skin can be seen, clearly demarcated from the surrounding skin. Based on the characteristics of the lesion and clinical presentation, the diagnosis is common warts. Common warts are a common skin condition usually caused by infection with the human papillomavirus (HPV). While common warts are typically benign, in some cases, treatment may be necessary to alleviate symptoms or prevent transmission to others. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

According to the symptoms and clinical manifestations, the diagnosis for the photo showing deep red blisters on the back of the hand is drug eruption, which is a skin inflammatory reaction caused by medication. Treatment for drug eruption involves discontinuing the offending medication and may include the use of antihistamines and topical medications to relieve symptoms.

This is a photo of the lower limbs showing significant hyperkeratotic papules and plaques in brownish color, consistent with the presentation of lichen amyloidosis. Lichen amyloidosis is a rare skin condition characterized by brownish patches or papules on the skin surface, commonly found on the lower limbs, with deposition of amyloid material in the affected tissue. Treatment mainly focuses on symptom management, such as local medication or laser therapy.

The photo shows symmetrical erythema, erosion, exudation, and ulceration on both knees, consistent with the clinical presentation of eczema. Eczema is a non-infectious inflammatory skin disease, often caused by allergies, genetic factors, or environmental factors. Treatment involves avoiding triggering factors, keeping the skin clean and moisturized, and using topical anti-inflammatory, antihistamine medications, or steroids. Close monitoring of the condition is necessary, and consultation with a dermatologist may be required for further assessment and treatment.

In this picture, there is a red patch on the baby's head, which appears smooth. Based on the symptoms described, a preliminary diagnosis of congenital skin dysplasia can be made. This condition may result from genetic factors, abnormal fetal development, or other causes leading to skin abnormalities. It usually does not cause pain or discomfort. It is recommended for parents to monitor the changes in the red patch regularly and seek treatment and management under the guidance of a pediatrician.

Based on the photo showing the brown-gray reticular patches on the chest, the diagnosis is consistent with Dowling-Degos disease. Dowling-Degos disease is a rare condition characterized by brown or grayish skin patches, commonly found on the trunk and limbs. While this condition is usually benign, there is a risk of malignant transformation, so regular follow-up and monitoring are recommended.

This is a picture of skin lesions on the face and neck, showing diffuse erythema and swelling, suggestive of granuloma faciale. Further investigation and evaluation may be needed to confirm the diagnosis.

This is a photo of the lower limbs showing scattered black and brown patches with some merging, surrounded by pigmentation. Based on these features, the diagnosis is drug eruption. Drug eruption is a skin condition caused by medications, typically presenting with rash, itching, pain, and other symptoms. It is important to discontinue the offending drug causing the drug eruption promptly and seek treatment under the guidance of a healthcare professional.

Small nodular lesions scattered in the nasal cavity, some of which are fused together, suggestive of sclerosing mucinous edema. Further pathological examination of nasal tissue is needed to confirm the diagnosis. Treatment usually involves anti-inflammatory and analgesic medications, as well as local steroid sprays. It is recommended to undergo pathological examination promptly to tailor individualized treatment plans.

This description indicates that the patient has patches on the skin with uneven color and uneven surface, with the lesion appearing white on the left side, light red in the center, and black-brown on the right side, with wart-like protrusions in the center. These characteristics of the lesions are consistent with the clinical presentation of squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically originates in the epidermis. It grows slowly but has invasiveness, making it prone to local and lymph node metastasis. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for the prognosis.

The patient presents with patchy purple lesions on the limbs, with superficial desquamation, suggestive of post-infectious changes. This condition may be caused by fungal, bacterial, or viral skin infection and requires further evaluation and treatment.

This is a photo of the face showing multiple round yellow-brown patches with a rough surface, consistent with a diagnosis of seborrheic keratosis. Seborrheic keratosis is a common skin condition characterized by localized overgrowth of keratin on the skin, leading to rough patches, often found on the face and scalp. It is usually a chronic condition that can be managed with topical keratolytic agents and corticosteroids.

Unevenly colored patchy papules with eccentric dark pigmentation may suggest basal cell carcinoma. A definitive diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma requires a comprehensive assessment of the patient's medical history and auxiliary examinations such as biopsy to confirm the diagnosis.

This is an image of facial skin lesion showing erythema, scaling, and keratotic follicular papules on the cheeks, peri-auricular area, and external auditory canal, which are consistent with the clinical presentation of folliculitis decalvans. Folliculitis decalvans is a chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by localized skin redness, swelling, itching, and desquamation. Treatment typically involves the use of topical steroids, antifungal medications, and keratolytic agents. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

This is an image of skin lesions on the chest, showing multiple dark red patches of various sizes, with a smooth surface. Some are slightly swollen while others are atrophied, consistent with the clinical presentation of scleroderma.

The patient shows symptoms of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, a rare genetic connective tissue disorder characterized by excessive skin stretching, leading to laxity of the skin on the face and other body parts, making it prone to wrinkles. In addition to facial features, patients may also have joint laxity, abnormal body posture, and other manifestations. Treatment mainly focuses on symptom management, including skincare and physical therapy. Regular follow-up and psychological support are also crucial.

Based on the multiple small red macules and papules seen in this chest photograph, the clinical presentation is consistent with contact dermatitis. Contact dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin condition typically caused by skin contact with allergens or irritants. Treatment involves avoiding the triggering substances, as well as the use of topical corticosteroids and antihistamines.

Reddish patches on the dorsal surface of the fingers, longitudinal stripes on the nails of multiple digits, and ecchymosis under the nails of the second and third digits on the right hand. These symptoms suggest a possible autoimmune disease, which requires further investigation and confirmation.

This is a photo showing skin lesions on the sole of the right foot, presenting as linear erythema. Combined with the clinical presentation and medical history, it is consistent with the clinical features of filariasis infection. Filariasis infection is a disease caused by parasitic worms in the skin, commonly found in tropical and subtropical regions. Patients may experience local itching, erythema, papules, and in severe cases, ulcers and inflammation. Treatment typically involves topical medications and symptomatic supportive care.

Based on the symptoms and appearance of the red circular patch with central erosion and thin scales covering the skin on the upper eyelid in the photo, the preliminary diagnosis is squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that originates from the keratinocytes of the epidermis. It typically presents as localized skin lesions with ulcers and scales at the affected area. A definitive diagnosis requires a comprehensive evaluation including clinical history, physical examination, and potential biopsy results.

This is a picture showing skin lesions on the tongue, with visible distinct horizontal lines. Based on the symptoms and signs, a preliminary diagnosis of fissured tongue is made. Fissured tongue is a common condition characterized by longitudinal and transverse cracks on the surface of the tongue, sometimes accompanied by pain or burning sensation. This condition is usually caused by metabolic imbalance of the epithelial cells on the tongue or local irritation. It is recommended to consult a dentist for further evaluation and treatment advice.

This photo shows multiple white papules on the patient's face surrounded by erythema, which are typical manifestations of acne. Acne is a common chronic inflammatory skin condition that typically occurs during adolescence due to chronic inflammation of hair follicles and their associated sebaceous glands. Acne lesions are mainly divided into non-inflammatory acne (such as closed comedones) and inflammatory acne (such as papules, pustules). Treatment options for acne include topical medications (such as creams containing benzoyl peroxide) and oral medications (such as antibiotics, retinoids). It is recommended for the patient to seek medical attention promptly for effective treatment.

This is a facial photo showing inflammatory redness and swelling of the eyelids, which are typical manifestations of dermatomyositis. Dermatomyositis is an inflammatory disease typically caused by a bacterial infection, leading to inflammation of the skin and muscle tissues. Patients may experience symptoms such as redness, swelling, pain, and fever. Treatment usually involves antibiotics and topical anti-inflammatory medications. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial in preventing complications.

The red patches, papules, and small pustules appearing on the forehead and nose may indicate seborrheic dermatitis or rosacea. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common skin inflammation that often occurs in the T-zone (forehead, nose, and chin), characterized by excessive sebum production leading to oily skin, blackheads, and inflammation. On the other hand, rosacea is a chronic inflammatory skin condition, presenting as facial redness, papules, and pustules, which can be easily confused and misdiagnosed. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for an accurate diagnosis and treatment plan.

This is a picture showing a linear erythema with vesicles and erosion on the lateral aspect of the calf, which is suggestive of contact dermatitis, possibly caused by contact with photosensitive substances. Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory reaction of the skin caused by exposure to irritants or allergens, common triggers include cosmetics, medications, metals, etc. Treatment usually involves avoiding contact with the offending substances and using topical corticosteroids.

This is a picture of skin lesion on the face and neck, showing red patches with clear boundaries, raised edges, central ulceration, erosion, and crusting, which are consistent with the clinical characteristics of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin malignancy that typically grows slowly, but if left untreated, it can invade deeper tissues. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for further diagnosis and treatment.

This is a photo of a child's hand. Irregular growths can be seen on the fingers, with a color consistent with the surrounding skin. Based on the characteristics of the lesion and clinical symptoms, the most likely diagnosis is squamous cell carcinoma. This type of cancer usually originates from the keratinocytes of the skin epidermis, showing characteristics of malignant proliferation, and further histopathological examination is needed to confirm the diagnosis and develop a treatment plan.

The photo shows diffuse purple-red papules on the back of the neck and shoulders of the patient, consistent with the diagnosis of dermatomyositis. Dermatomyositis is an autoimmune disease characterized by purple-red papules on the skin and symptoms such as muscle pain and weakness.

This photo shows large patches of bright red spots on the neck with clear boundaries, indicating symptoms of sunburn. Sunburn is an inflammatory skin condition caused by prolonged exposure of the skin to ultraviolet light. Symptoms include redness, itching, pain, and peeling of the skin. Treatment typically involves avoiding sun exposure, using topical medications such as steroids and emollients, and taking oral antihistamines. It is recommended for the patient to avoid sun exposure, use sunscreen, and seek professional medical treatment promptly.

The patient presents with dense distribution of uniform round papules or nodules on the skin in the image, with relatively smooth surfaces that do not merge significantly, consistent with the typical presentation of cutaneous amyloidosis. Cutaneous amyloidosis is a rare systemic amyloidosis characterized by deposition of amyloid material in the skin and other organs, often associated with genetic factors, manifesting clinically as skin lesions and systemic symptoms.

In this photo, we can see scattered papules on the face. Based on the symptoms and signs, the preliminary diagnosis is herpes simplex. Herpes simplex is a common skin infection caused by the herpes simplex virus, characterized by herpetic lesions. Further laboratory tests are recommended for confirmation.

Swelling and black spots on the skin at the base of the index finger nail, along with cone-shaped black streaks on the nail plate and black spots at the fingertip, are indicative of melanoma. Melanoma is a malignant tumor that usually develops from melanocytes and can appear on the skin as irregularly colored, poorly defined black patches. This is a photo of the armpit area showing multiple irregular black papules, consistent with the clinical presentation of melanoma. Melanoma is a malignant tumor that usually originates from melanocytes in the skin. If not treated promptly, it can spread to other parts of the body. It is recommended that the patient seek medical attention for further evaluation and treatment.
The lesion presents as nodules with a waxy, semi-transparent appearance, raised and curled edges, central ulceration, consistent with the clinical features of basal cell carcinoma.

A flesh-colored, bag-like growth, suggestive of a skin tag (acrochordon or fibroepithelial polyp), which is a common benign skin tumor typically caused by overgrowth of subcutaneous connective tissue. Skin tags are usually painless, with a smooth surface, flesh-colored or slightly yellow, and soft in texture. This is an image showing red patches and scales on the hand, with clear borders and alignment of the lesions with the skin lines, consistent with a diagnosis of nummular eczema. Nummular eczema is a chronic, recurrent skin condition characterized by localized lesions. The exact cause is unclear, but it is believed to be related to immune reactions and typically requires treatment with corticosteroids.

The patient had flaky erythema on the dorsum of his feet, with central regression and slightly raised edges. It is consistent with the performance of granuloma annulare, and dermatopathological examination is recommended for further diagnosis.

This is a photo showing red papules scattered on the trunk, which could be indicative of seborrheic dermatitis based on the characteristics and location of the lesions. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin condition, usually caused by various factors such as excessive sebum production, fungal or bacterial infections, etc. Treatment typically involves topical medications such as antifungal agents, corticosteroids, and oral medications if necessary. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for personalized treatment plans by healthcare professionals.

This is an image showing skin lesions on the scrotum, with multiple millet to mung bean-sized skin-colored papules at the base of the scrotum, consistent with the clinical presentation of hidrocystoma. Hidrocystoma is a common skin condition, typically caused by blockage of sweat gland ducts leading to cysts in the skin. This condition is usually benign and does not typically require special treatment. However, if there are symptoms or cosmetic concerns, surgical or laser treatment may be considered.

The image shows densely distributed erythematous patches of varying sizes with fine scales, consistent with the clinical presentation of pityriasis rosea. This is a chronic recurrent skin condition characterized by red patches with scales and associated itching. The exact cause is not well understood but may be related to immune system abnormalities, genetic factors, and environmental triggers. This photo shows an irregular-shaped ulcer on the nasal bridge, with some parts crusted and some parts showing red erosion. The photo shows scattered wheals on the patient's back, some are bright red and some are dark red. This is an image of skin lesions on the lower limbs, showing multiple black rashes on the extension side of the lower limbs. Based on the clinical presentation and pathological examination results, the preliminary diagnosis for the lesion on the lower limb showing ulceration, erosion, ulcer, and scab is squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is a type of skin cancer that typically originates from the epidermis and can be confirmed through a tissue biopsy.

The photo shows a dark brown patch with unclear edges and relatively regular shape, which is consistent with the clinical presentation of a developmental mole. Ring-shaped brown patches on the scalp with uneven color. Consider: Skin tumors need to be investigated, and Becker's nevus is highly likely. A combination of medical history, dermoscopy and histopathological examination is required to confirm the diagnosis.

Based on the description of the symptoms, the patient presents with ring-shaped erythema on the lower limbs, with central regression and clear edges. This condition may be caused by erythema centrifugum or tinea corporis, further investigations are needed for a definitive diagnosis.

Diagnosis: This is a typical presentation of folliculitis. Folliculitis is a common skin condition usually caused by bacterial infections. The photo shows extensive thickened erythematous plaques with scaling at the ankle, which is indicative of tinea pedis. Centrifugal annular erythema is a common skin condition characterized by red annular patches on the skin, with central fading and slightly raised and darker edges. This image shows multiple white papules and plaques on the skin, consistent with the diagnosis of skin calcinosis. Skin calcinosis is a rare genetic disorder characterized by the deposition of calcium salts in the skin, resulting in white or brown papules and plaques, typically seen in childhood. In Chinese medicine, this is a common skin condition typically found on the fingers, hands, knees, etc. Common warts are caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection and usually do not cause serious symptoms, but they can be bothersome aesthetically. This photo shows a single pale red plaque with irregular shape and scaling on the surface, which is consistent with the clinical presentation of squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically originates from the squamous cells in the epidermis. Early squamous cell carcinoma often presents as abnormal patches on the skin, and as the condition progresses, ulceration, bleeding, and other symptoms may occur. On this photo of the penis, large areas of depigmented patches with clear borders and irregular shapes can be seen, indicative of vitiligo. This photograph shows dense light yellow elevated patches on the extensor side of the elbow in the upper limb, consistent with the characteristic presentation of xanthoma. Xanthoma is a relatively common chronic skin disease, typically manifesting as yellow or brownish-yellow papules or plaques of varying sizes and colors on the skin surface, with the main lesion sites being the extensor surfaces and the face. These patches are usually firm in texture and may sometimes be itchy or painful, potentially affecting aesthetics when severe. Treatment options include the use of topical corticosteroid creams, phototherapy, cryotherapy, among others.

The diagnosis is seborrheic keratosis, a common skin condition characterized by irregular slightly elevated patches on the skin surface with excessive keratinization. Granuloma annulare is a rare skin condition characterized by small, swollen, red bumps or nodules with clear borders and smooth surfaces on the skin. Further evaluation and treatment may be necessary to determine the specific diagnosis and develop an appropriate treatment plan.

Basal cell carcinoma is diagnosed based on the observation of brownish patches, central depression, irregular shape, unclear borders, and dilated blood vessels around the lesion. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically originates from the basal cells beneath the epidermis, presenting with features similar to those described above. The image shows a brown-black nodule with uneven pigmentation, smooth surface, semi-spherical shape, and clear borders, consistent with a diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically grows slowly but can have potential invasiveness.

On this photo, there is an irregular black-brown patch with uneven color and irregular borders, typical features of in situ melanoma. In situ melanoma is a non-malignant skin condition that typically forms on the surface of the skin, but if left untreated, it may progress to malignant melanoma. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for further evaluation and treatment.

This photo shows extensive faint red rash with unclear borders around the eyes, possibly indicating periorbital dermatitis. A detailed skin examination is recommended to rule out other conditions and determine the best treatment plan.

This is a picture of skin lesion on a finger, showing dark red patches around the nail, with uneven surface and clear boundaries, and keratosis at the fingertip, which is indicative of possible squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is a type of skin cancer that usually arises from the keratinocytes in the skin's top layer, and it may present with irregular colors and shapes, and in severe cases, ulcers and bleeding. This is an image of the upper body of a female showing numerous red papules and edematous plaques on the upper limbs and upper back with clear borders. These symptoms are typical of acute febrile neutrophilic dermatosis, which may be accompanied by fever, joint pain, and malaise. The etiology of this skin condition is complex and may be related to factors such as infection, rheumatic diseases, etc. Treatment typically involves antibiotics, corticosteroids, and symptomatic management such as local anti-inflammatory and anti-edema measures.

In this picture, scattered red patches with scales can be seen on the trunk, which is a typical presentation of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by red patches covered with silvery scales. It is often caused by immune system abnormalities and commonly affects areas such as joints, scalp, and trunk.

Diagnosis: The patient presents with scattered purple-red and yellow papules, some showing papillary changes on the surface, and some are dome-shaped, consistent with the clinical features of lymphangioma. Lymphangioma is a benign tumor caused by congenital abnormal lymphatic development, which can occur in the skin and mucous membranes. It is usually painless and non-pruritic, most often solitary. Based on the palm-sized ulcer with filthy yellow-black surface and raised edges resembling a dike, the patient is diagnosed with squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is a common malignant skin tumor that typically originates from the epidermis, grows slowly but has invasive properties. Confirming the diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma requires a histological examination to determine the pathological type for appropriate treatment planning.

Facial black papules may be caused by seborrheic keratosis or melanocytic nevi, further investigation is needed for a definitive diagnosis.
