Melanocytic nevi, on the other hand, are pigmented patches on the skin, mostly benign, but it is important to monitor for signs of malignancy.

The photo shows multiple papules and comedones consistent with hair follicles on the skin surface, which is indicative of acne. Acne is a common chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by papules, pustules, and nodules around hair follicles. It is commonly seen in adolescents during puberty and is associated with factors such as hormonal changes, excessive sebum production, and bacterial infection.

Multiple scattered dark red nodules with partial surface desquamation can be seen in the image, consistent with nodular prurigo. This is a common skin condition characterized by red, itchy nodules on the skin. The etiology of nodular prurigo is not well understood and may be related to immune system abnormalities. Treatment typically involves topical corticosteroids and antihistamines. Regular follow-up visits are crucial in managing this condition.

The described features correspond to the clinical presentation of squamous cell carcinoma of the skin. Squamous cell carcinoma is a common malignant tumor of the skin, usually originating from squamous cells of the epidermis. The lesion appears as dark red, infiltrative nodules with symptoms such as ulceration, bleeding, necrosis, and crusting on the surface. A definitive diagnosis requires a tissue biopsy to determine the pathological type and grade, in order to develop an appropriate treatment plan.

This is a photo of facial skin lesions showing pale red infiltrative plaques with surrounding ring-shaped elevations, consistent with the features of sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis is a chronic systemic disease that can affect multiple organs in the body, including the skin, eyes, and lungs. Common skin manifestations include skin lesions, nodules, and erythema. This condition is often caused by immune system abnormalities and requires comprehensive treatment to control symptoms and progression.

The photo shows multiple round pale red patches with raised edges and smooth surfaces on the back of the hand, consistent with the characteristics of granuloma annulare. Granuloma annulare is a common skin condition that typically presents as localized circular or oval raised patches on the skin, commonly found on the hands, feet, and neck. This is an image showing scattered vascular lesions on the face, consistent with the diagnosis of tuberous sclerosis. This is a photo showing irregular red patches on the face that merge with each other. This presentation is characteristic of telangiectasia, a condition typically caused by dysfunction of the capillaries, leading to their dilation and accumulation of blood on the skin surface.

Based on the characteristics of the lesion and clinical manifestations, the diagnosis of actinic dermatitis can be confirmed. Actinic dermatitis is an inflammatory skin disease caused by photosensitivity of the skin, commonly found in sun-exposed areas. Treatment may involve avoiding sunlight exposure and using topical corticosteroids.

The patchy redness and scattered scabs around the anus are typical manifestations of perianal eczema or perianal intertrigo. Perianal eczema is a type of skin inflammation, usually caused by moisture, friction, or allergies, presenting as redness, itching, and erosion. Perianal intertrigo, on the other hand, is a skin inflammation caused by friction around the anus, with common symptoms including itching, pain, and bleeding. An accurate diagnosis requires a combination of the patient's medical history, clinical manifestations, and necessary examinations. Treatment options include keeping the area dry and clean, using anti-inflammatory medications, and avoiding irritating factors.

Hyperkeratotic nodule with surrounding collar-like changes, suggestive of verrucous proliferation. Further evaluation is recommended for confirmation. Verrucous proliferation is a common skin condition, often caused by infection with human papillomavirus (HPV), appearing on both skin and mucous membranes, mostly benign but can potentially undergo malignant transformation.

The patient had scattered light red flat papules with smooth surfaces, consistent with cutaneous amyloidosis. Cutaneous amyloidosis refers to a chronic skin disease in which amyloid protein is deposited in normal skin without affecting other organs. The cause of the disease is not completely clear. Amyloid is deposited in the papillary layer of the dermis and causes the disease.

This is a photo showing multiple erythematous patches on the trunk, presenting as round patches that partially merge with exudative crusts around them. Erythema multiforme is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by red patches that may be itchy and painful. It is common in young to middle-aged adults and has complex etiology, possibly related to immune dysregulation. Diagnosis relies on the patient's medical history, clinical presentation, and skin histopathology examination results. Treatment options may include topical medications and phototherapy to alleviate symptoms.

Based on the characteristics and appearance of the lesion in the photo, it can be diagnosed as a melanocytic nevus. Melanocytic nevus is a common skin condition typically caused by localized overgrowth of melanocytes. In most cases, it is benign and does not require treatment. However, regular monitoring for any changes is important.

This is an image showing skin lesions on the toenail, characterized by nail pitting, thickening underneath, loss of luster, and whitening, which are consistent with the features of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that typically presents with scales, itching, and inflammation. This condition can affect the skin, nails, joints, among other areas.

This is a photo showing multiple purple-red nodules along the edge of the foot, with clear boundaries with the surrounding tissues and smooth surface, consistent with the clinical features of Kaposi's sarcoma. Kaposi's sarcoma is a rare malignant tumor that typically originates in the skin and soft tissues and is prone to occur in distal parts of limbs such as hands and feet.

Based on the description of the photo showing multiple nodules on the chest with smooth surfaces protruding from the skin and some nodules merging into pseudopodia, the diagnosis of keloid nodules is suggested. Keloid nodules are a condition characterized by overgrowth of scar tissue during the skin healing process, often seen after trauma or surgery. Further dermatological examination and evaluation are recommended to establish an appropriate treatment plan.

Based on the description of the facial photo showing a half-spherical red nodule with central keratinization and scales, this is a typical presentation of squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically originates from the epidermis, exhibiting invasive and metastatic potential.

This is a photo of the nose showing skin thickening, hypertrophy, and erythema. Based on the symptoms, a preliminary diagnosis of rosacea with nasal hypertrophy is made, further confirmation and treatment plan are needed.

Scattered red macules and papules on the cheeks are consistent with post-inflammatory changes from acne. Acne is a common chronic skin condition that can lead to inflammatory responses, resulting in residual red macules and papules that may take some time to completely fade.

This is a picture of skin lesions on the face, showing multiple lice and nits at the root of the upper eyelid eyelashes, diagnosed as pediculosis, also known as lice infestation. Pediculosis is a parasitic disease caused by lice infestation on the human skin, commonly found on the head and eyelashes.

This is a photo of the scrotum showing scattered raised hard nodules on the surface, some with smooth surfaces and white tips, consistent with the clinical presentation of scleroderma. Scleroderma is a rare connective tissue disease characterized by fibrotic proliferation of the skin and other organ tissues, which can lead to dysfunction and deformities when severe. It is recommended that the patient seek medical attention promptly for further diagnosis and treatment.

This is a picture showing multiple uneven dark red patches with scales on the skin of the lower leg, consistent with the clinical presentation of follicular red papules. Follicular red papules is a chronic inflammatory skin condition commonly seen on the scalp, face, neck, and upper chest, but can also occur in other areas. The exact cause of follicular red papules is not clear, but it may be related to factors such as genetics, immune system, and environment.

The patient presents with pale red papules with surface scales and a Koebner phenomenon. These symptoms are suggestive of lichen planus, a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by localized thickening of the skin, color changes, surface scaling, and itching.

The scattered elliptical erythema with the long axis parallel to the skin lines is characteristic of pityriasis rosea. This skin condition is commonly seen in children and young adults and may be associated with allergic reactions. The diagnosis of pityriasis rosea is mainly based on clinical presentation and typically does not require special laboratory tests.

This is a photo of an infant's face showing red, crusted patches on the forehead, between the eyebrows, and on the cheeks, with adherent scales, consistent with seborrheic dermatitis. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common skin inflammation characterized by facial redness, seborrhea, and inflammation, often seen in infants, young children, and adolescents. It is recommended to avoid friction, keep the skin clean, and use gentle skincare products for management. In cases of severe or persistent symptoms, it is advisable to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.
Black papule with central depression, suggestive of a melanocytic nevus. Further evaluation is needed to confirm the diagnosis.

This is a photo of a skin lesion showing faint red patches with central lesion fading and presenting with annular changes, typical of Lyme disease. Lyme disease is a tick-borne illness caused by the bacterium Borrelia burgdorferi, commonly seen in individuals who have been bitten by ticks. The disease often presents with early skin manifestations such as rashes and skin lesions, which if left untreated can lead to serious complications involving the joints, nervous system, and other organs.

This is a picture showing several light red infiltrative nodules on the skin of the penis with clear borders, consistent with the clinical presentation of scabies. Scabies is an infectious disease caused by the scabies mite, often presenting with skin rash and itching, commonly seen in skin folds and areas of skin-to-skin contact. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for diagnosis and treatment.

The child has oval-shaped brown patches on the chest, consistent with the features of café au lait spots. This type of skin lesion is usually caused by abnormal pigmentation of the skin and often present at birth. Café au lait spots are generally benign, but in some cases, they may be associated with genetic conditions. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for further evaluation and management.

The photo shows multiple white and pale yellow granular papules on the skin surface, with a smooth texture, consistent with the clinical features of milia. Milia is a common skin condition characterized by small, varying in size, granular papules, commonly seen on the face, neck, and chest. Possible causes include follicular keratosis and sebaceous gland dysfunction. Treatment mainly involves the use of topical agents such as keratolytics and topical anti-inflammatory medications.

The photo shows skin lesions on the hand, presenting as polygonal purple-red flat papules with a shiny surface. Based on the lesion characteristics, the preliminary diagnosis is lichen planus. Lichen planus is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by purple-red flat papules, often accompanied by itching and inflammatory reactions.

The patient presents with red patches distributed on the extensor surfaces of both upper limbs, with central target-like lesions, which are characteristic of erythema multiforme. Erythema multiforme is a common skin condition that typically presents with irregularly shaped red patches or plaques, often with central target or radial lesions. The etiology of erythema multiforme is diverse and may be related to factors such as infection, drug allergies, and autoimmune reactions. Diagnosis is based on clinical presentation and skin biopsy results.

This is a photo of the face showing redness, pigmentation changes, and large ulcers on the cheeks. Based on the symptoms described, the preliminary diagnosis is dyschromatosis symmetrica hereditaria, a chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by pigmentation changes and ulcer formation.
This is a common skin lesion that typically does not require special treatment. It is recommended to monitor any changes regularly and consult a dermatologist for routine check-ups.

Based on the image, the patient's facial skin appears dry with numerous densely distributed brownish papules. A deep ulcer with a diameter of approximately 1*1.5cm is visible on the left side of the nose, consistent with the clinical presentation of terra firma-forme dermatosis. Terra firma-forme dermatosis is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry skin, brownish papules, and in severe cases, ulcer formation. Treatment involves avoiding irritating cosmetics and cleansers, maintaining skin cleanliness and moisture, avoiding sun exposure, and using topical or oral corticosteroids if necessary. It is important to monitor for signs of infection at the ulcer site and schedule regular follow-up appointments to assess disease progression.

This is an image of a skin lesion on the lower jaw, showing a large area of red or purple vascular dilation on the skin, consistent with port-wine stain. Port-wine stain is a type of congenital vascular malformation characterized by localized vascular dilation and abnormal twisting of blood vessels, commonly seen on the face and neck. Most patients present with this condition at birth, and the color of the lesion may darken with age, with a tendency to bleed. Treatment options for port-wine stain include laser therapy and other modalities.

This is a roughly circular lesion with a diameter of about 1.5 cm, characterized by a central ulceration oozing crusts with visible vascular dilation around it. Based on the morphological features of the lesion and clinical presentation, combined with the results of histopathological examination, the clinical diagnosis is squamous cell carcinoma.

The photo shows thickening and lichenification of the skin, supporting the diagnosis of neurodermatitis. Neurodermatitis is a chronic inflammatory skin disease typically characterized by skin changes due to abnormalities in the nervous system. This condition often presents with localized skin thickening, lichenification, itching, and pain.

The patient's symptoms are related to a shin skin ulcer, characterized by a black-yellow wound with slightly raised edges, possibly indicative of a skin soft tissue infection or skin tumor. Further physical examination and necessary laboratory tests are needed for a definitive diagnosis.

The patient presents with large red patches on the skin without scaling, suggestive of a hemangioma. Hemangiomas are common vascular developmental anomalies typically formed by the proliferation of endothelial cells. These skin lesions appear as red, purplish-red, or dark red patches or nodules and usually do not exhibit scaling.

The case presents as oval-shaped red patches with thick yellow adherent scales. Considering the clinical presentation and pathological features, the diagnosis of cutaneous lupus erythematosus is likely. Cutaneous lupus erythematosus is a subtype of lupus erythematosus, an autoimmune disease that primarily affects the skin, manifesting as red patches, scales, rashes, and other symptoms. Treatment typically involves the use of corticosteroids and immunosuppressants.

Basal cell carcinoma is diagnosed based on the appearance and clinical presentation of the lesion, which shows an ulcer on the skin of the head above the left ear, with raised edges resembling beads, a scab in the center, and slight bleeding at the base. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically grows slowly, but if not treated promptly, it can invade surrounding tissues and organs.

This is a photo of the hand showing scattered circular red patches, which, combined with the clinical symptoms and signs, are consistent with the characteristics of lupus erythematosus. Lupus erythematosus is a chronic autoimmune disease commonly seen in young women, which can affect multiple organ systems such as the skin, joints, and kidneys. The diagnosis of lupus erythematosus usually requires a comprehensive assessment of clinical manifestations, laboratory tests, and histological examination.

This is a photo of the eye socket. It can be seen that the skin around the eye socket is thickened and there is some desquamation. Based on the medical history and physical examination, the preliminary diagnosis is mucinosis, a rare condition that typically leads to abnormal growth of the skin and mucous membranes.

This is a photo of skin lesions on the right thigh. It shows scattered dark red papules, with the papules in the groin area merging to form plaques, covered with white fine scales. The diagnosis is pityriasis rosea. This condition is a chronic, recurrent skin inflammation commonly seen on the trunk, neck, and inner aspects of the limbs, presenting with red patches, papules, and scales on the skin. Treatment typically involves topical corticosteroids and moisturizers. In cases of severe symptoms or recurrent episodes, prompt medical attention should be sought for further treatment and management.

The patient presents with pale brownish hemispherical papules with a smooth surface and umbilication, raising suspicion of a skin lesion or a tumor originating from the hair follicle glands. To confirm the diagnosis, a histopathological examination is recommended. This diagnostic procedure involves examining tissue samples under a microscope to determine the nature and malignancy of the lesion. Once the pathology report is available, the doctor will plan further treatment accordingly.

The photo shows four red papulonodules on the abdomen, suggesting nodular disease. Nodular disease is an inflammatory condition that typically presents with multiple nodular lesions in tissues, affecting various organs. Diagnosis of this condition requires a combination of clinical presentation, imaging studies, and histological findings. Treatment usually involves a combination of medications and surgical interventions.

Multiple red papules of varying sizes on the head are present, suggesting a possibility of skin tumor that requires further investigation for confirmation. It is recommended to combine detailed medical history, physical examination, and histopathological examination to establish a definitive diagnosis.

The newborn had massive hemorrhagic macules, maculopapular rashes, and scabs on the face, neck, trunk, and limbs, which were diffusely distributed, consistent with the clinical manifestations of Langerhans histiocytosis. The diagnosis requires a combination of family history, clinical manifestations, and histopathological examination. . Langerhans histiocytosis is often associated with multisystem damage.

This is a picture of skin lesions on both hands, with purple-red patches visible on the backs of the hands and crusts on the surface, consistent with the clinical manifestations of neutrophilic dermatosis.

This is a common skin condition, usually caused by bacterial infection. Folliculitis can be classified as superficial or deep, with symptoms including local skin redness, swelling, pain, and sometimes pus discharge. Treatment depends on the type and severity of folliculitis, and may involve topical antibiotic creams or oral antibiotics. It is important to avoid squeezing the pimples during treatment to prevent worsening the infection. Seeking timely medical attention can help alleviate symptoms and prevent complications.
Oval-shaped smooth-surfaced nodule may be a skin cyst or an epidermoid cyst, further evaluation with soft tissue ultrasound and pathological examination is needed for a definitive diagnosis.

This is a photo of the lip area showing erosion, thin crusts, and fine fissures, consistent with the clinical presentation of cheilitis. Cheilitis refers to inflammatory conditions affecting the skin around the lips, often caused by factors such as dryness, cold weather, allergies, or infections. Treatment for cheilitis typically involves keeping the lips moisturized, avoiding irritants, and using topical medications. Timely diagnosis and treatment can help alleviate symptoms and prevent worsening of the condition.

This is a photo of the trunk showing scattered red papules with pustules on the surface, indicative of folliculitis. It is likely caused by a bacterial infection. Folliculitis is inflammation of the hair follicles and common symptoms include redness, swelling, pain, and pustules. Treatment typically involves topical antibiotics and maintaining local hygiene. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for appropriate treatment.

The photo shows a well-defined dark purple edematous patch with a central large blister, which is a typical presentation of fixed drug eruption. Fixed drug eruption is a type of drug allergy reaction that typically occurs within days of drug exposure, presenting as a skin inflammatory reaction, possibly with blisters and itching. Treatment includes discontinuing the offending drug, topical corticosteroids, and antihistamines. In severe cases, oral corticosteroids or other immunosuppressants may be needed.

On the trunk, there are skin-colored papules without hair, suggesting a possible diagnosis of dermatofibroma. Dermatofibroma is a common benign skin tumor that is usually asymptomatic and presents as subcutaneous nodules or papules, often found on the trunk, neck, and axilla. Diagnosis of dermatofibroma typically requires a combination of clinical presentation and dermatoscopy to rule out other similar conditions. Treatment usually involves observation, but surgical excision may be considered if there are any discomfort. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for further evaluation and confirmation.

Based on the characteristics of the lesion and clinical presentation, the preliminary diagnosis for the brown, rough skin lesion on the scalp in the photo is flat warts. Flat warts are a common chronic skin condition, typically presenting with well-defined borders, changes in skin color, and thick, hardened texture, commonly found on the scalp, neck, and extremities. Treatment options include topical medications and local care, with severe cases possibly requiring oral medications or other treatment modalities. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and management.

Pigmented urticaria is a common skin condition characterized by the presence of brown or black papules on the skin. The onset of this disease may be related to factors such as pigment deposition and immune reactions. Treatment typically involves local medication and avoiding triggering factors. Regular follow-ups to monitor changes in the condition are important after diagnosis.

The photo shows diffuse erythema and red wheals in the groin area, consistent with the clinical presentation of urticaria pigmentosa. This condition is likely caused by an allergic reaction. Further investigation into the patient's allergy history and triggers is recommended to tailor a personalized treatment plan.

This is a photo showing scattered red plaques with scales on the hand, which is a typical presentation of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic, relapsing autoimmune disease characterized by redness, swelling, scaling of the skin, and itching. Treatment options for psoriasis include topical medications, phototherapy, and systemic medications. Patients also need to avoid trigger factors such as stress, fatigue, and infections. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for controlling the progression of the disease.

This is an image showing skin lesions around the eyes, with pea to soybean-sized skin-colored papules, consistent with the clinical presentation of a syringoma. Syringoma is a common skin condition typically caused by the dilation of the eccrine sweat ducts in the deeper layers of the skin. It can appear on the face, neck, and other areas. Syringomas are usually benign and do not typically require treatment. However, if symptoms affect appearance or cause discomfort, surgical or laser treatment may be considered.
Small papules on the nipple, located in the central umbilicus. Consideration of skin tumor pending investigation. Further histopathological examination is needed for a definitive diagnosis.

This is a photo showing clear and irregular white patches on the foot and ankle, consistent with the clinical presentation of vitiligo. Vitiligo is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by depigmentation of the skin, commonly seen on the face, hands, feet, and other areas. Treatment for vitiligo may include laser therapy, topical corticosteroids, oral immunosuppressants, among others.

The described features of the lesion, including oval dark red patches, nodules of varying sizes around the skin lesions with raised edges, are consistent with the clinical presentation of nodular disease. Nodular disease is an inflammatory condition that typically affects multiple organs, especially lymph nodes, lungs, eyes, and skin. Skin manifestations can present as erythematous plaques, nodular eruptions, and other lesions.

This is a common skin condition that usually occurs during adolescence. It is caused by the blockage of hair follicles due to overactive sebaceous glands, leading to bacterial infection and inflammatory reactions. Acne lesions typically appear on the face, neck, chest, and back, presenting as red papules, pustules, nodules, and cysts. Treatment options include topical medications, oral medications, phototherapy, and other comprehensive approaches.

Patients present with a network of purple streaks at the hairline, suggesting a possibility of subcutaneous bleeding. Further investigations are recommended to confirm the diagnosis. Possible causes include trauma, coagulation disorders, or vascular diseases. Diagnosis requires a comprehensive assessment of the patient's medical history, physical examination, and necessary laboratory tests.

This photo shows swelling and suppuration at the nail groove of the finger, consistent with the clinical presentation of paronychia. Paronychia is an infectious disease of the soft tissues around the nail, commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus. Treatment includes topical antibiotic ointment application, oral antibiotics if necessary, and keeping the affected area clean and dry. In severe cases, surgical intervention may be required. Prompt treatment can prevent further spread of infection and alleviate pain symptoms.
